I am the heavy, golden sigh of summer, the month where the sun lingers long in the sky until the twilight turns a bruised purple. I am the hum of cicadas in the thick heat, the taste of salt on skin after a long day at the lake, and the slow, syrupy drip of honey in the afternoon. I carry the weight of late-blooming sunflowers and the frantic joy of backyard barbecues, stretching my humid breath across the land until the first faint whisper of autumn begins to cool my fever.

I do not wish to be anyone else, for I am the golden peak of the year, the heavy, honey-thick heat that hangs in the air like a physical weight. I am the shimmering haze over parched fields, the long, lazy afternoons where time stretches until it feels endless, and the sudden, bruised purple of a summer thunderstorm. I am the taste of salt on skin, the scent of mown grass, and the slow, amber decline of light as I hold onto the sun just a moment longer before the first whispers of autumn begin to stir. To be any other month would be to lose this lingering, breathless ache of fullness—this vibrant, sweltering pulse of life before the cooling shadows arrive.

I want to remain that bridge between the vibrant peak of summer and the first cooling sigh of autumn, a steady heartbeat of amber light and sun-drenched memories that never has to yield to the falling leaves.
